elements.sent-2411-001: Recent proposals suggested quantum clock interferometry for tests of the Einstein equivalence principle.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, atom interferometric models often include relativistic effects only in an ad hoc fashion.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here, instead, we start from the multi-particle nature of quantum-delocalizable atoms in curved spacetime and generalize the special-relativistic center of mass (COM) and relative coordinates that have previously been studied for Minkowski spacetime to obtain the light-matter dynamics in curved spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, for a local Schwarzschild observer located at the surface of the Earth using Fermi-Walker coordinates, we find gravitational correction terms for the Poincaré symmetry generators and use them to derive general relativistic COM and relative coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-005: In these coordinates we obtain the Hamiltonian of a fully first-quantized two-particle atom interacting with the electromagnetic field in curved spacetime that naturally incorporates special and general relativistic effects.
elements.sent-2411-006: Light-pulse atom interferometers are high precision instruments that have demonstrated their effectiveness in a wide range of applications, including measurements of gravitational acceleration~\cite{Kasevich1991,Peters1998,Peters1999 , rotation~\cite{Lan2012 , and Newton's gravitational constant~\cite{Rosi2014 , as well as in field applications~\cite{Barrett2016,Wu2019,Templier2022,Stray2022 and mobile gravimetry~\cite{Wu2019 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: The most accurate determination of the fine structure constant to date was achieved using atom interferometry~\cite{Parker2018,Morel2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Proposals for interferometer schemes have also been put forward to test the universality of gravitational redshift~\cite{Roura2020,Ufrecht2020 and the universality of free fall~\cite{Ufrecht2020 , as well as gravitational wave detection~\cite{Dimopoulos2008b,Dimopoulos2009,Graham2016 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Prototypes, whose construction recently began, could be sensitive to ultra-light dark matter signals~\cite{Geraci2016,Arvanitaki2018,Badurina2023a , making them promising candidates as testbeds for gravitational wave antennas~\cite{Abe2021,Bertoldi2021,Badurina2021 based on atom interferometry.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: These applications show that there is a lot of interest in gravitational measurements via atom interferometry.
elements.sent-2411-011: Most theoretical descriptions are based on a ad hoc addition of special-relativistic and gravitational effects such as the mass defect and gravitational potentials.
elements.sent-2411-012: First approaches to include general relativistic (GR) effects in atom interferometer phases have already be done by Dimopoulos et al.~\cite{Dimopoulos2008 and Werner et al.~\cite{Werner2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: A post-Newtonian description of a two-particle atom (e.g. a proton and an electron) in weakly curved spacetime -- including external electromagnetic fields -- has been given by Schwartz et al.~\cite{Schwartz2019,Schwartz2020b , which is an extension of the work of Sonnleitner et al.~\cite{Sonnleitner2018 to curved spacetimes.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Sonnleitner et al.~\cite{Sonnleitner2018 showed in their paper that by using special-relativistic corrected center-of-mass (COM) and relative coordinates~\cite{Osborn1968,Close1970 they can decouple the dynamics of internal and external degrees of freedom in such a way that the remaining cross-terms can be interpreted as the mass defect, i.e., the total mass of the atom depends on the internal state.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Schwartz et al.~\cite{Schwartz2019,Schwartz2020b , however, found an additional cross-term between the internal and external dynamics induced by the gravitational field that does not vanish using these special-relativistic COM and relative coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-016: There are, though, two issues that one needs to consider for a proper treatment of the matter.
elements.sent-2411-017: The starting point of Schwartz et al. in Refs.~\cite{Schwartz2019,Schwartz2020b is the Eddington-Robertson parametrised post-Newtonian metric, c.f.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This metric is an excellent starting point for examining deviations from GR.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, it is radially symmetric, which it is because it describes physical situations from the perspective of an observer at the center of the sourcing mass, e.g., the Earth.
elements.sent-2411-020: For an accurate description of physical phenomena as seen by a realistic observer, we first have to describe everything within a proper reference frame, i.e., the Fermi-Walker frame~\cite{Nesterov1999,Manasse2004,Klein2008,Klein2009,Poisson2011,Klein2012,Martin-Martinez2020,Perche2021,Perche2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Fermi-Walker coordinates (FWC) are characterized by a tetrad basis attached to an observer, where the timelike basis vector is parallel to the four-velocity of the observer's worldline and the spatial triad does not rotate.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper we will restrict ourselves to non-rotating observers.
elements.sent-2411-023: Generalizations would employ generalized FWC~\cite{Kajari2009,Kajari2011,Llosa2017 , which have been used, e.g., in the context of relativistic Sagnac interferometry by Kajari et al. in Refs.~\cite{Kajari2009,Kajari2011 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Already in the 1950s the relativistic dynamics of systems of multiple particles aroused the interest of physicists: The dynamics of systems of particles with and without internal interactions have been shown to be relativistically invariant when the condition of invariant world lines is dropped~\cite{Thomas1952 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Further, the separability of internal and external degrees of freedom has been investigated for non-interacting particles~\cite{Dirac1949,Macfarlane1963 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Internal interactions were inserted into the rest mass by Bakamjian et al.~\cite{Bakamjian1953 , c.f., the mass defect -- one of the aforementioned ad hoc insertions.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Afterwards, Foldy~\cite{Foldy1961 confirmed the results of Bakamjian et al.~\cite{Bakamjian1953 by considering a system of a fixed number of directly interacting particles.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: When separating the internal and external dynamics, one usually introduces COM and relative coordinates and momenta.
elements.sent-2411-029: When dealing with relativistic particles, these coordinates known from undergraduate textbooks are relativistically corrected, as shown by Osborn and Close for systems of vanishing total electric charge~\cite{Osborn1968,Close1970 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: These relativistically corrected coordinates have shown to be extremely useful to decouple the internal and external degrees of freedom of a two-particle atom~\cite{Sonnleitner2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Martínez-Lahuerta et al. found a generalization of these coordinates for systems of non-vanishing total charge~\cite{Martinez-Lahuerta2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: While Osborn and Close~\cite{Osborn1968,Close1970 found the relativistic internal coordinates via a singular Gartenhaus-Schwartz transformation, Liou found a more elegant way to determine the relative coordinates via a unitary transformation~\cite{Liou1974 .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Ultimately, there is the work of Krajcik and Foldy where special-relativistic COM and relative coordinates are calculated for composite systems with arbitrary internal interactions~\cite{Krajcik1974 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: All these have one thing in common: they make use of the generators of the Poincaré group, i.e., the symmetry group of flat spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-035: In the case of curved spacetime, however, the symmetry generators do not look the same as in flat spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-036: Consequently, the relativistic COM and relative coordinates of Refs.~\cite{Osborn1968,Close1970,Krajcik1974,Liou1974,Martinez-Lahuerta2022 should be modified when dealing with gravitation.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The goal of this paper is to find these GR corrections for weakly curved spacetimes and use them to obtain a Hamiltonian describing a two-particle atom interacting with external electromagnetic fields, in analogy to Schwartz et al.~\cite{Schwartz2019,Schwartz2020b , yet for a realistic local observer on Earth.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In the following we shortly describe the structure of this paper: In Sec.~\ref{Sec: Metric in FWC we recapitulate the basics of FWC and use them to expand the Schwarzschild metric around the worldline of a static observer located at the equator of the Earth up to second order in FWC.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In Sec.~\ref{Sec: Generators of Symmetry Group we use this metric to find gravitational correction terms for the well-known Poincaré symmetry generators up to first order in $\epsilon=R_S/R_E$ via the Killing equations, where $R_S$ is the Schwarzschild radius and $R_E$ is the radius of the Earth.}
elements.sent-2411-040: With these corrected symmetry generators in hand, we can then calculate the GR corrections for the COM and relative coordinates in Sec.~\ref{Sec: Generalized COM and relative Coordinates .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Finally, in Sec.~\ref{Sec: Two-Particle Dynamics in Curved Spacetime we use the metric of Sec.~\ref{Sec: Metric in FWC and the generalized COM and relative coordinates of Sec.~\ref{Sec: Generalized COM and relative Coordinates to calculate -- in analogy to Schwartz et al.~\cite{Schwartz2019,Schwartz2020b -- the full Hamiltonian describing a two-particle atom with vanishing total charge in curved spacetime including external electromagnetic fields.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: We then conclude with a summary and a discussion of our results in Sec.~\ref{Sec: Conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-043: In order to obtain physical observables corresponding to an experiment witnessed by an observer, we have to describe the dynamics in the proper coordinate frame~\cite{Nesterov1999,Manasse2004,Klein2008,Klein2009,Poisson2011,Klein2012,Martin-Martinez2020,Perche2021,Perche2022,Kajari2009,Kajari2011,Llosa2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Let us assume that we have a four-dimensional manifold $\mathcal{M}$ with a Lorentzian metric $g^\prime_{\mu\nu (y^\alpha)$ given in a priori coordinates $y^\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Subsequently this will be chosen to be the Schwarzschild metric given in spherical coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-046: These coordinates, however, do not describe physical phenomena as seen by a realistic observer.
elements.sent-2411-047: Instead, we have to find a coordinate system attached to the worldline $\sigma^\alpha(\tau)$ of the observer, parametrized by its proper time $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-048: For this we choose the FWC $x^\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The goal of this section is to provide a short introduction to the Fermi-Walker frame which will serve as the basis for the generalized Osborn coordinates in curved spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-050: To do so we firstly have to define the orthonormal tetrad basis $e_{(\alpha) ^\mu(\tau)$ attached to the observer.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Note, that the indices in brackets are tetrad indices in contrast to coordinate indices.
elements.sent-2411-052: They are raised and lowered by the Minkowski metric \mbox{$\eta=\mathrm{diag (-1,1,1,1)$}.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The time coordinate in FWC is given by the proper time, i.e., $x^0 = \tau$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Let us define the basis at some given time $\tau_0 = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-055: We can then choose the timelike tetrad to be the tangent vector to the worldline: $e_{(\alpha) ^\mu(0) = \mathrm{d}\sigma^\alpha/\mathrm{d}\tau (0)=u^\alpha(0)$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The remaining three spatial tetrads can be chosen accordingly so that they build a right-handed basis for the spatial tangential space and fulfill the orthonormality condition \begin{equation}\label{eq: orthonormality condition} g^\prime_{\mu\nu}e^\mu_{(\alpha)}(0)e^\nu_{(\beta)}(0) = \eta_{(\alpha\beta)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-057: To take into account the motion of the observer in curved spacetime, the tetrads have to be correctly transferred from one tangential space to another.
elements.sent-2411-058: That is, they have to be Fermi-Walker transported along the worldline $\sigma^\alpha(\tau)$: \begin{equation}\label{eq: Fermi Walker transport} e_{(\alpha);\nu}^{\mu}u^\nu - \frac{1}{c^2}\left(u^\mu a_\beta - a^\mu u_\beta\right) e_{(\alpha)}^\beta= 0,\end{equation} where $a_\beta$ is the four-acceleration.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq: Fermi Walker transport} e_{(\alpha);\nu}^{\mu}u^\nu - \frac{1}{c^2}\left(u^\mu a_\beta - a^\mu u_\beta\right) e_{(\alpha)}^\beta= 0,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-059: Fermi-Walker transport ensures an instantaneous rest frame that is non-rotating under the observer's motion, and reduces to parallel transport for geodesics.
elements.sent-2411-060: Furthermore, it follows that the tetrads continue to be orthonormal at all times $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-061: It is easy to see that the four-velocity $u^\alpha$ automatically fulfills the transport equation, Eq.~\eqref{eq: Fermi Walker transport , so that the timelike tetrad can be chosen to be $e_{(0) ^\alpha(\tau)=u^\alpha(\tau)$ for all times $\tau$, c.f., Fig.~\ref{fig: FWC .}}}

elements.sent-2411-062: For the observer's wordline we define the normal neighborhood $\mathcal{N _{\sigma^\alpha(\tau)}$ such that all points in that neighborhood can be connected to $\sigma^\alpha(\tau)$ via a unique geodesic.}

elements.sent-2411-064: To describe a point $p$ via FWC, we firstly have to find the rest surface $\Sigma_\tau$ that contains $p$ and find the corresponding time $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-065: After that we can assign the spatial coordinates $x^i$ so that \begin{equation}p = \exp_{\sigma^\alpha(\tau)}(x^i e_{(i)}(\tau)),\end{equation} where $\exp_{\sigma^\alpha(\tau)}$ is the exponential map at the point $\sigma^\alpha(\tau)$.
elements.sent-2411-066: In this Fermi-Walker frame, the spatial distance between a point $x^\alpha$ and the observer is then given by $r=\sqrt{\delta_{ij x^ix^j}$.}

elements.sent-2411-070: Let us now particularize our setting, without loss of generality, to the case of an observer on the Earth's equator.
elements.sent-2411-071: We may model this via a static observer in a Schwarzschild spacetime, provided the experiments take place over a sufficiently small time period compared to the time scale associated with the angular frequency of the rotating Earth, i.e. \mbox{$\omega_Earth\approx 7.29\times10^{-5}\mathrm{s ^{-1}$}.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Therefore, the Schwarzschild metric is sufficiently suited to obtain first order gravitational effects in atomic experiments.
elements.sent-2411-073: However, one could also apply our method to, e.g., the Kerr metric.

elements.sent-2411-075: In detail, let us consider the Schwarzschild metric that belongs to a static, spherically symmetric mass distribution in spherical coordinates: \begin{equation}\label{eq: Schwarzschild metric} g^\prime_{\mu\nu} = diag\left(-\left(1-\frac{R_S}{r}\right),\left(1-\frac{R_S}{r}\right)^{-1},r^2,r^2 \sin^2\theta\right),\end{equation} where $R_S=2GM_E/c^2$ is the Schwarzschild radius and $M_E$ the Earth's mass.
elements.sent-2411-076: We choose a static observer on (without loss of generality) the $x$-axis with a distance $R_E$, corresponding to the radius of the Earth, to the center of the mass distribution.
elements.sent-2411-077: The worldline in a priori coordinates $\mathsf{y =(ct, r, \theta,\phi)$ with coordinate time $t$ is then given by \begin{equation}\label{eq: worldline observer} \sigma^\alpha(t) = \left(c t, R_E, \frac{\pi}{2}, 0\right).\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-082: To determine the relativistic COM and relative coordinates for an atom made of constituent particles in curved spacetime, we need to find the generators corresponding to the spacetime symmetry group attached to the oberserver's trajectory in the FWC~\cite{Osborn1968,Close1970,Liou1974,Krajcik1974 .}
elements.sent-2411-083: In flat spacetime for inertial motion of the observer, this corresponds then to the Poincaré group~\cite{Dirac1949 .}
elements.sent-2411-084: By finding the irreducible representation and the Casimir operators of the symmetry group, one obtains a global definition of a particle.
elements.sent-2411-085: The first Casimir operator $P^2$, i.e., the square of the four-momentum, defines the mass, and the second, i.e., the square of the Pauli-Lubanski pseudovector $W^2$, defines the spin of the particle~\cite{Wigner1939,Bargmann1948,Braathen1969 .}
elements.sent-2411-086: For a system of non-interacting particles, the individual Poincaré generators are added independently, corresponding to the respective Hilbert space~\cite{Dirac1949,Macfarlane1963 .}
elements.sent-2411-087: When adding interactions, the Casimir operators still exist but can become more complex as interactions can affect the symmetry properties and the definitions of conserved quantities.
elements.sent-2411-088: When allowing for non-inertial motion, the global notion of particles does no longer hold as can be seen, for instance, by the Unruh effect~\cite{Unruh1976 .}
elements.sent-2411-089: For an accurate description of spin in non-inertial frames, corrections that account for the non-inertial nature of the frame need to be included.
elements.sent-2411-090: As a consequence, the Casimir operator associated with the spin in local coordinates is no longer invariant under Lorentz transformations~\cite{Singh2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-091: In fact, the spin depends on the corresponding Lorentz frame even for free particles~\cite{Peres2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Since the spin of a particle is defined locally with respect to a local inertial frame, it changes under local Lorentz transformations~\cite{Terashima2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-093: The Casimir operator defining spin, i.e. $W^2$, possesses gravitational and frame dependent contributions~\cite{Singh2009,Singh2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-094: A possible approach is the use of local Lorentz transformations to investigate the spin properties of (possibly interacting) spin-$1/2$ particles in curved spacetime~\cite{Terashima2004,Alsing2009,Basso2021,Singh2007,Singh2009,Singh2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-095: Here however we will perform a perturbative ansatz to modify the known generators of the Poincaré group.
elements.sent-2411-096: In this section, we determine the local Killing symmetries and map them to a perturbed Poincaré algebra to order $\mathcal{O (\epsilon)$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: This leads us to a local definition for the generators and the Casmir operators, cf., Refs.~\cite{Terashima2004,Alsing2009,Basso2021,Singh2007,Singh2009,Singh2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-098: Consequently, there is only a local definition of a particle.
elements.sent-2411-099: The generators of the spacetime symmetry group are the Killing vectors $\xi^\mu\partial_\mu$ whose components satisfy the Killing equations \begin{equation}\label{eq: Killing equation} \xi_{\beta;\alpha}+\xi_{\alpha;\beta}=0\Leftrightarrow\partial_\beta\xi_\alpha+\partial_\alpha\xi_\beta-2\Gamma^\gamma_{\alpha\beta}\xi_\gamma=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-100: In flat spacetime the Killing equations are easily solved.
elements.sent-2411-101: Since the Christoffel symbols vanish in this case, the Killing equations reduce to \begin{equation}\label{eq: Killing equation flat spacetime} \partial_\beta\xi_\alpha+\partial_\alpha\xi_\beta=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-102: The Killing vectors must then have the form \begin{equation}\xi_\alpha = a_\alpha + b_{\alpha\beta}x^\beta\end{equation} with $b_{\alpha\beta =-b_{\beta\alpha}$ while $a_\alpha$ can be chosen arbitrarily.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Therefore, there are ten linearly independent solutions: four translations in space and time through $a_\alpha$ and three rotations and boosts through $b_{\alpha\beta}$.
elements.sent-2411-104: Solving the Killing equations exactly for curved spacetimes is in general very cumbersome.
elements.sent-2411-105: We derive now GR corrections to the special relativistic generators for the metric derived in the previous section, Eq.~\eqref{eq: Metric in FWC , by inserting the ansatz \begin{equation}\xi_\alpha = a_\alpha + b_{\alpha\beta}x^\beta + c_{\alpha\beta\gamma}x^\beta x^\gamma + O(x^3)\end{equation} into the Killing equations, Eq.~\eqref{eq: Killing equation , where $a_\alpha = a_{SRT,\alpha + a_{GR,\alpha}$, $b_{\alpha\beta = b_{SRT,\alpha\beta + b_{GR,\alpha\beta}$ and $c_{\alpha\beta\gamma = c_{GR,\alpha\beta\gamma}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: For the case of observers in more general spacetimes, semi-analytical results may follow analogously.

elements.sent-2411-119: We can now proceed to calculate gravitational corrections to the relativistic COM and relative coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-120: The underlying principle is that the sum of the single particle symmetry generators has to have the same form in relativistic COM coordinates as the single particle generators~\cite{Osborn1968,Close1970,Liou1974,Krajcik1974 .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Although this works for an arbitrary number of particles, we restrict our calculations to hydrogenoid atoms, consisting of two particles with, e.g., an electron and a proton.
elements.sent-2411-122: In this way we arrive at ten equations that can be used to find the relativistic COM position $\vec{R}$, the total momentum $\vec{P}$, spin $\vec{S}$ and mass $M$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Then we can insert the ansatz \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{eq:COM ansatz} \vec{R} =\vec{R}_{NR}+\vec{R}_{SRT}+\vec{R}_GR\; \epsilon, \vec{P} =\vec{P}_{NR}+\vec{P}_{SRT}+\vec{P}_GR\; \epsilon, \vec{S} =\vec{S}_{NR}+\vec{S}_{SRT}+\vec{S}_GR\; \epsilon, M =M_{NR}+M_{SRT}+M_GR\; \epsilon,\end{equation} \end{subequations where the subscripts $NR$, $SRT$ and $GR$ labels the non-relativistic (NR) solutions and the special (SRT) and GR corrections, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Expanding all ten equations in $\epsilon$, we see that the zeroth order is already solved by the NR and the SRT solutions that are already known~\cite{Osborn1968,Close1970,Liou1974,Krajcik1974 .}
elements.sent-2411-125: We can then use the first order equations to calculate the gravitational correction terms.
elements.sent-2411-126: Since we are only interested in terms up to the order $c^{-2}$ and the Schwarzschild radius $R_S$ is already of this order, we only need to solve the equations up to the zeroth order in $c^{-1}$.

elements.sent-2411-136: Having determined the gravitational corrections to relative and COM coordinates, in the next step we want to study the dynamics of a localized matter system in the presence of gravity and light.

elements.sent-2411-138: As a simple example, we consider in the following a hydrogenoid two-particle atom, consisting of, e.g., an electron and a proton, coupled to internal and external electromagnetic fields and gravitation.
